summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a galaxy cluster?

Galaxy clusters are the largest gravitationally bound structures in the universe.

What is the Local Group?

The Local Group contains the Milky Way, Andromeda, Triangulum, and approximately 80+ smaller galaxies.

What is a supercluster?

Superclusters span hundreds of millions of light-years but are not necessarily gravitationally bound.

What is the intracluster medium (ICM)?

The ICM is heated to tens of millions of degrees and emits X-rays, containing more baryonic mass than the galaxies themselves.

What is a cosmic void?

Voids are the universe's most underdense regions, typically 30-300+ million light-years across.

What is the Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect?

CMB photons passing through hot cluster gas get a small energy boost, creating a detectable signature.

What is the virial theorem?

The virial theorem allows astronomers to estimate the total mass of a cluster from the velocities of member galaxies.

What is ram pressure stripping?

Galaxies plowing through the ICM can have their gas stripped away, quenching star formation.

What is gravitational lensing by clusters?

Massive clusters can create dramatic arcs and multiple images of more distant galaxies, revealing both cluster mass and background sources.

A galaxy falling into a cluster loses its gas tail behind it. What is this process?

The galaxy's gas is swept away by the pressure of the intracluster medium, like wind removing water from a car.

A background galaxy appears as a long, thin arc behind a massive cluster. What is causing this?

The cluster's gravity bends light from the background galaxy, distorting it into an arc (or even a full Einstein ring).
